title: Dog Seizures After Head Trauma: Immediate Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Seizures following a head injury in dogs are a critical emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is vital and how to document the episode.
content_html:
Understanding Post-Traumatic Seizures in Dogs

A seizure following a head injury—such as a fall, a car accident, or being struck by an object—is a clinical sign of traumatic brain injury. This occurs when the physical impact causes the brain to swell, bleed, or suffer from oxygen deprivation, which disrupts the normal electrical activity of the brain and leads to a seizure.

Urgency Level: High (Critical Emergency)

If your dog experiences a seizure after any form of head trauma, this is a high-priority emergency. You should seek immediate veterinary care at the nearest 24-hour emergency hospital. Post-traumatic seizures often indicate rising intracranial pressure or internal bleeding, both of which require professional stabilization and medication to prevent permanent neurological damage or fatality.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

If it is safe to do so without delaying your trip to the vet, capturing a short video or a photo of your dog during or immediately after the episode can be incredibly helpful for the medical team. This visual evidence allows the veterinarian to assess the type of seizure, the severity of the muscle contractions, and the dog’s level of consciousness, which helps them prioritize your pet's care the moment you arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: What should I do while my dog is seizing?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog on a flat surface away from stairs or sharp furniture. Do not put your hands near their mouth, as they may bite reflexively, and move them only if they are in immediate danger.

faqs.question: Can a seizure happen long after the initial head injury?
faqs.answer: Yes, while some seizures happen immediately, others can occur hours or even days later as brain swelling or inflammation progresses. Always monitor your dog closely for 72 hours after any head trauma.

faqs.question: What are other signs of brain trauma to look for?
faqs.answer: In addition to seizures, look for pupils of different sizes, difficulty walking, unusual lethargy, or vomiting, all of which indicate a need for urgent veterinary evaluation.
